Today we're going to be talking about optimizing your ads when you run CBO. CBO stands for Campaign Budget Optimization and it lets Facebook do most of the work for you. So as you can see in this illustration, the whole point of using this kind of campaign is to let Facebook reallocate your money to the best performing ad sets so that you have the highest number of conversions possible. There are some times when Facebook does make some weird decisions though, so we are going to have to steer Facebook away from certain things. So the first thing that you'll want to do when learning to steer Facebook away from things is choose your campaign that you're wanting to look at. Make sure you check that box so you're only looking at those ads and go to the ad set level of that campaign. I'm mainly going to be looking at our results, our reach, our cost per result, and the amount spent. But there are other things that you could look at that will help you as well. So the first thing that I want to do is I want to ask myself two questions about my ads. One, have people seen them? And two, have they fizzled out? So in order to answer those questions, I'm going to sort my cost per result so that my highest priced cost per result, so my highest priced lead, is at the top. From here, I'm going to be asking myself mainly, have people actually seen my ad? And in order to find that out, I'm going to be looking at the reach. I would consider anything with a reach in between 3,000 to 5,000 to be a good enough reach to shut something off. So as I scroll down and look at my reach compared to my higher prices, I can see that a lot of these ads have not really seen the light of day. And so I don't want to preemptively turn anything off because these ads could end up being phenomenal for me. For example, if only two more dollars were put into this ad and five people clicked on this one in particular, I'd immediately have five to six dollars leads. So we don't want to turn something off too quickly. So make sure that you are making logical decisions and looking at all the variables before shutting something off. This ad right here has had about a reach of 3,000 and I'm not really liking the $20 cost per result when I have these results down here that are so much better. So you can see that I've turned this one off. These three on the other hand, I'm going to watch over the next few days or few weeks, depending on what and how Facebook spends my money, and just make sure that they don't spend too much and that the reach doesn't go up without the price going down here. So the next thing that I'm going to do is I'm going to sort my cost per result the other way. I'm going to ask my other question. Have my ads fizzled out? So sometimes our ads can be deceiving and they can look like they have a really good cost per result. But when you look at a different forecast, so when you look at how the ads have been performing, let's say for the past week, they'll show you completely different sets of data. So what I would suggest for you to do is look at your ads with a three to seven day forecast in order to make educated decisions. I'm just going to show you an example of why I'm bringing this up by showing you a 30 day forecast on these ads, which all look like they're doing phenomenally. If I change this forecast to 30 days, you can see what has happened. So you want to make sure that you're looking at a three to seven day forecast as you look at your ads. That way you know if any of your high performers have fizzled out. So if any of the high performers have, you'll want to turn them off as well. By turning them off, what you're doing is you're forcing Facebook to reallocate the money and test other ad sets. So this only works if you have other ad sets for it to test. So from here on out, if you turn an ad set off, you need to also make a new one. So for every ad set and ad you turn off, you'll want to duplicate, add in new creative, possibly new copy, and then publish a new ad. So I hope this gives you an idea of how to optimize with CBO. And if you have any questions, don't hesitate to ask. Tech support is always here for you. I hope that helps.
